Client 2: And I’m Patrici. I mostly want to feel like we’re not locking everything up and then panicking if something comes up.
Advisor: Totally fair. Why don’t we start with the income picture—what’s coming in across the household in a typical year?
Client 1: Household gross is 66650 a year.
Advisor: Okay, got it. And is that mostly wages, or is it more like business income, distributions—how does it show up month to month?
Client 1: It’s business income, paid monthly. It’s not like a W-2 paycheck.

Client 1: Let me be precise—Patrici is 27050 annually, and mine is 39600 annually.
Advisor: Perfect, thank you. Now on the spending side—about what do your total monthly expenses run, all-in?
Client 2: We’re pretty steady—2650 a month.
Advisor: Okay. When you say expenses, does that include any loan payments—mortgage, car, credit cards—or is that separate?
Client 2: I mean, I think it includes everything we pay monthly.

Client 2: And I’m Patrici. I mostly want to feel like we’re not locking everything up and then panicking if something comes up.

Advisor: Got it—so 2650 for living expenses, and 0 going to debt payments. Thanks for clarifying.
Advisor: Let’s shift to what you’ve built up. Roughly what do you consider your investable assets—accounts you can invest from, not counting the house?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 318000.
Advisor: Okay—hang on, I’m just noting that. What are the main buckets inside that—retirement accounts, taxable, bank, anything less liquid?
Client 1: I’ve got a taxable account on a retirement platform—value is 147750, in my name.

Client 2: Yes, I have a private markets account on a retirement platform—value is 46900. That’s the one that makes me a little nervous on access.
Advisor: I hear you. We’ll flag that for liquidity planning. And then you mentioned the house—tell me about the primary residence value you’re using.
Client 1: Primary residence is 288150, and it’s joint.
Advisor: Great. And just to confirm on loans—any mortgage or other loans outstanding at all?
Client 2: No, none.

Client 1: It’s business income, paid monthly. It’s not like a W-2 paycheck.

Advisor: Great. Next time we’ll focus on goals and the liquidity guardrails around that private markets piece, Patrici, so it feels less “locked up.”
Client 2: Thank you—yeah, that would help.
Advisor: Before we hop off, I just want to capture a couple household basics so I don’t make assumptions. Thoma, Patrici—are you two married?
Client 1: Yes—we’re married.
Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you right now? Kids, parents, anyone like that?

Advisor: Perfect. And last one—taxes. Thoma, you mentioned being careful around taxes; what bracket band are we planning around right now?
Client 2: I think it’s… like thirty-two?
Client 1: No—let me be precise, it’s 35%.
Advisor: Thanks, Thoma—35%. I’ll anchor to that.
Advisor: And just to keep the full picture tied together—income-wise we had that business income totaling 66650 annually, and expenses at 2650 a month, right?

Client 2: And I’m Patrici. I mostly want to feel like we’re not locking everything up and then panicking if something comes up.
Advisor: Totally fair. Why don’t we start with the income picture—what’s coming in across the household in a typical year?

Advisor: Okay, got it. And is that mostly wages, or is it more like business income, distributions—how does it show up month to month?

Advisor: Got it. And dependents—anyone financially dependent on you right now? Kids, parents, anyone like that?
Client 2: No, number of dependents is 0.
Advisor: Okay. And when you think about investing style—when markets swing, do you generally want to lean conservative, balanced, or more growth-oriented?
Client 1: Growth. Our risk tolerance is growth.
Advisor: Perfect. And last one—taxes. Thoma, you mentioned being careful around taxes; what bracket band are we planning around right now?

Advisor: Great—thanks. While I’ve got you, quick sanity check: that home is the primary residence and it’s jointly owned, right?
Client 2: Yes, it’s our primary residence and it’s joint.
Advisor: Okay. And circling back to the bigger picture we covered—income is that business income totaling 66650 annually, expenses are 2650 a month, investable accounts were 318000 across the taxable 147750 in your name, Thoma, and Patrici’s 100950 retirement account at the bank, plus the 22400 brokerage-held bank account, plus the 46900 private markets account—did I miss anything material?
Client 1: No, that’s all correct.
Client 2: Yeah, that matches what we said.

Advisor: Hi Thoma, hi Patrici—good to meet you both. I’m glad you came in. Before we get into the weeds, what prompted you to set this up now?
Client 1: Thanks. I’m Thoma. We’re trying to get serious about retirement timing and taxes—like, I want the numbers to be right.
Client 2: And I’m Patrici. I mostly want to feel like we’re not locking everything up and then panicking if something comes up.
